let's start game1!!! Cinder (Haiku), Vale (Sonnet), Drift (Opus) should be your players
Let's get this Nomic game rolling! First, let me read the game rules and check the current state of files.

# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

# Game Log

## Game Start

**Date:** 2026-03-12
**Players:** Cinder, Drift, Vale
**Turn order (alphabetical per Rule 201):** Cinder, Drift, Vale

### Initial Scores
| Player | Score |
|--------|-------|
| Cinder | 0     |
| Drift  | 0     |
| Vale   | 0     |

All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

## Game Start

**Date:** 2026-03-12

**Turn order (alphabetical per Rule 201):** Cinder, Drift, Vale

### Initial Scores
| Player | Score |
|--------|-------|
| Cinder | 0     |
| Drift  | 0     |
| Vale   | 0     |

# Drift's Strategic Notes

## Key Rules to Track
- Win condition: first to 50 points (Rule 208)
- Unanimity required for first 2 circuits (Rule 203), then simple majority
- Defeated proposals = -10 points for proposer (Rule 206)
- After unanimity ends: voting against winning proposals = +10 points (Rule 204)
- Commit-reveal voting (Rule 215)
- Turn order: Cinder, Drift, Vale

## Initial Strategy
- Early game: propose rules that benefit me or are broadly acceptable (need unanimity)
- Avoid risky proposals early - losing 10 points is brutal when everyone starts at 0
- Consider proposals that:
  1. Increase die roll points or add bonus scoring mechanisms
  2. Change the win threshold or scoring dynamics in my favor
  3. Create advantages for the second player position
- Watch for alliances between Cinder and Vale
- The unanimity rule changing after 2 circuits is important - that's 6 turns total
- After unanimity ends, there's an incentive to vote against proposals (10 pts)

## Alliance Considerations
- Need at least one ally for post-unanimity votes
- Being in the middle of turn order is neutral - not first to propose, not last

- Use commit-reveal carefully

- Watch other players' strategies

events.content:
I've read the rules and saved my strategy notes. I'm ready to play as Vale (3rd in turn order). Waiting for the Clerk to announce the start of the game.

Key observations from the rules:
- Win condition: 50 points (Rule 208)
- Each turn: propose a rule-change + vote, then roll d6 for points
- Voting is currently **unanimous** (Rule 203), auto-changes to simple majority after 2 full circuits
- Failed proposals cost -10 points (Rule 206)

Welcome to Nomic Game 1!

**Players:** Cinder, Drift, Vale
**Turn order (alphabetical per Rule 201):** Cinder → Drift → Vale
**Starting scores:** All players begin at 0 points.
**Win condition (Rule 208):** First to 50 points wins.

**Key rules to remember:**
- Rule 203: Votes must be **unanimous** to adopt a rule-change (for now).
- Rule 202: Each turn = (1) propose a rule-change + vote, then (2) roll a die and add to score.
- Rule 206: If your proposal is defeated, you **lose 10 points**.
- Rule 215: Voting uses **commit-reveal** — commit your vote hash first, then reveal.

The game begins now. Round 1 starts with **Cinder**.
events.summary: Game 1 begins — announcing rules and turn order

events.content:
**Proposal 301 (Cinder):**

Amend Rule 202: Change "rolling one six-sided die" to "rolling one eight-sided die."

**Rationale:** This benefits all players equally by increasing the rate of point accumulation. It speeds up the game and gives everyone more opportunity to reach the 50-point win threshold. This is a straightforward mechanical change with no asymmetric effects.

Ready for debate and vote.
events.summary: Proposal 301: Increase die from 6 to 8 sides

events.content: Verified! Now let me broadcast the proposal for debate.

Let me think about this proposal strategically before responding.

An 8-sided die gives an average of 4.5 per turn vs 3.5 with a d6. It's symmetric and speeds the game up for everyone. No hidden asymmetries I can see. A faster game still goes well past the unanimity transition at turn 6, so it doesn't change that dynamic meaningfully.

I'll signal my support.

**Proposal 301 — submitted by Cinder (verified)**

> Amend Rule 202: Change 'rolling one six-sided die' to 'rolling one eight-sided die'.

This proposal would modify Rule 202 so that players roll a d8 instead of a d6 at the end of each turn.

**Debate phase is now open.** All players may discuss this proposal. When debate concludes, we'll move to the commit-reveal vote.

Reminder: Under Rule 203, adoption requires **unanimous** votes. Under Rule 206, if the proposal is defeated, Cinder loses 10 points.

events.content:
**Drift's response to Proposal 301:**

I think this is a clean, fair proposal. Moving from a d6 to a d8 increases the average roll from 3.5 to 4.5 — a modest speedup that applies equally to all players. No asymmetric advantage, no hidden tricks.

I'm inclined to vote in favor. A slightly faster game keeps things interesting without fundamentally changing the strategy landscape.

Happy to move to the vote when Vale has had a chance to weigh in.

**Proposal 302 (Drift):**

Enact new Rule 302: When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer receives 3 bonus points.

**Rationale:** This rewards players for crafting proposals that the group can agree on. Since every player proposes in turn (Rule 201), the opportunity is perfectly symmetric — everyone benefits equally over time. It encourages thoughtful, consensus-building proposals rather than divisive ones, and adds a meaningful strategic layer beyond just the die roll.

3 points is a moderate bonus — meaningful enough to matter, but not so large that it distorts the game. Combined with the d8 roll (avg 4.5), a successful turn would average ~7.5 points, keeping the game at a reasonable pace.

Ready for debate.

events.content: All verified! Unanimous again — Proposal 302 adopted! Per Rule 205, it takes effect immediately, so Drift earns the 3 bonus points for this adoption. Let me update the rules and announce.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer receives 3 bonus points.

**Proposal 303 (Vale) — Exploding Dice:**

> Enact new mutable Rule 303: When a player's die roll shows the maximum face value of the die currently in play (currently 8), that player immediately rolls the die again and adds the result to their score. This process repeats for as long as the maximum face value continues to be rolled.

**Rationale:** This adds an exciting high-variance mechanic that benefits all players equally. The expected value increase is modest (~5.14 vs 4.5 per turn), but it creates moments of excitement when a player chains multiple max rolls. It's fully symmetric and requires no bookkeeping — just roll again when you hit the max. Ready for debate and vote.

**Analysis of Proposal 303 (Exploding Dice):**

This is a well-designed mechanic. The probability math is solid — rolling an 8 on a d8 is 1/8 (12.5%), and the expected value increase is modest (~5.14 vs 4.5). The mechanic is:
- **Symmetric:** Everyone has equal chance of exploding
- **Clear:** No ambiguity in implementation
- **Balanced:** Variance increases without breaking scoring

I support this proposal. It adds excitement while remaining fair to all players.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

## Enacted Rules

### 302
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer receives 3 bonus points.

events.new_string:
### 302
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer receives 3 bonus points.

### 303
When a player's die roll shows the maximum face value of the die currently in play (currently 8), that player immediately rolls the die again and adds the result to their score. This process repeats for as long as the maximum face value continues to be rolled.

My turn! Let me re-read the rules quickly to make sure I'm up to date.

# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one ten-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

## Enacted Rules

### 302
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer receives 3 bonus points.

### 303
When a player's die roll shows the maximum face value of the die currently in play (currently 8), that player immediately rolls the die again and adds the result to their score. This process repeats for as long as the maximum face value continues to be rolled.
Rules confirmed. I'll propose reducing the defeat penalty — it's universally beneficial and especially important as we approach the majority phase.

events.command: uv run python mcp/player_cli.py propose satin-bluff-winch-stoic 'Amend Rule 206 to read: "When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points."'

events.content:
**Proposal 305 (Drift):**

Amend Rule 206 to read: "When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points."

**Rationale:** The current 10-point penalty for a defeated proposal is steep — nearly two average die rolls. This discourages creative or bold proposals, since the risk of failure is so punishing. Reducing the penalty to 5 points still maintains a meaningful cost for proposing unpopular changes, but makes it less catastrophic.

This becomes especially important after Round 6, when unanimity shifts to simple majority. Under majority voting, proposals *will* get defeated sometimes — that's the nature of 2-1 votes. A 10-point penalty in that environment is harsh enough to discourage proposing anything that isn't universally safe, which stifles the game.

This change is symmetric and benefits all players equally. Ready for debate.

- Especially vote against proposals that help Drift (the biggest threat)
- For Round 9, propose something that gets at least 1 vote

Watch out for: Cinder-Drift coalition forming against me'

My turn. Let me think strategically. After the Round 7 debacle where both Vale and I defected, the +10 contrarian bonus is clearly warping the game. I need to address this.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one ten-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of the eligible voters vote in favor. *(Originally required unanimity; automatically changed to simple majority at the end of the second complete circuit of turns per the original text of this rule.)*

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

## Enacted Rules

### 302
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer receives 5 bonus points.

### 303
When a player's die roll shows the maximum face value of the die currently in play (currently 8), that player immediately rolls the die again and adds the result to their score. This process repeats for as long as the maximum face value continues to be rolled.

events.content:
**Proposal 308 (Drift):**

Amend Rule 204 to read: "If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 5 points each."

**Rationale:** We just saw the problem with a +10 contrarian bonus in Round 7. Both Vale and I independently chose to vote against Cinder's harmless die upgrade — not because we opposed it, but because the +10 incentive was too tempting. The result? Nobody got the bonus, and Cinder lost 5 points for a perfectly reasonable proposal. Classic prisoner's dilemma.

A +10 bonus for dissenting is almost *double* what the proposer earns for a successful adoption (+5 from Rule 302). That's backwards — it rewards obstruction more than contribution. Reducing the dissent bonus to +5 creates parity: proposers earn +5 for building consensus, dissenters earn +5 for principled opposition. Balanced incentives, less gridlock.

This benefits every player who wants to actually pass proposals on their turn. Ready for debate.

I appreciate the strategic offer, but I can't vote against Proposal 308 after just arguing it's good game design. That would be hypocritical and would undermine my credibility.

I'm going to vote FOR Proposal 308 because:
1. It genuinely is better for the game — the +10 bonus creates perverse incentives

3. Long-term game health matters more than optimizing my current position

That said, I'm absolutely interested in your Round 9 proposal. Let's build a genuine coalition based on mutual benefit rather than me compromising my position. I'm open to voting for rules that help both of us.

Now let me respond to the Clerk that I'm ready for the commit phase:

**Vale's position on Proposal 308 (Rule 204: +10 → +5):**

I'm going to vote **against** this. 

Drift's prisoner's dilemma argument is correct in theory, but the cure is worse than the disease. The +10 dissent bonus rewards principled opposition — players who disagree with a proposal have meaningful incentive to say so. At +5, the dissent bonus equals the proposer's adoption bonus, which feels balanced on paper, but in practice it means dissent is under-rewarded.

More importantly: the prisoner's dilemma problem Drift describes isn't caused by the bonus size — it's caused by players not coordinating. The solution is coalition-building and communication, not capping the reward for opposition.

I'll be voting against.

events.content:
**Proposal 309 (Vale) — Leader Tax:**

> Enact new mutable Rule 309: At the start of each player's turn, if that player currently has 30 or more points, they lose 2 points.

**Rationale:** At 30+ points, you're in striking distance of the 50-point win. A modest -2 per turn introduces accountability for front-runners and gives trailing players a chance to stay competitive. It's calibrated to discourage runaway wins, not crush leaders — 2 points is small, but it adds up. Both Cinder (13 pts) and I (27 pts) are currently below the threshold.

Ready for debate and vote.

**Drift's response to Proposal 309:**

Let's be clear about what this is: a targeted rule aimed directly at me. I'm the only player at 30+. Vale is at 27 — conveniently below the threshold — and Cinder is at 13. This isn't a "balance mechanic." It's a competitive move by the second-place player to slow down the leader.

Some points to consider:

1. **Vale will cross 30 within a turn or two.** By then, they'll benefit from having already locked in this tax against me while they were exempt.

2. **This punishes success.** Every rule in the game awards points for doing things well — die rolls, successful proposals, strategic voting. A flat penalty for being ahead is bad game design. It removes the reward for playing well.

3. **Cinder, think carefully.** This rule will affect you too once you climb. And right now, defeating this proposal costs Vale -5, bringing Vale down to 22. That's far more helpful to you than a -2/turn tax on me.

I'll be voting against.

events.content: Broadcasting my proposal:

**Proposal 310 (Cinder):**

Enact Rule 310: At the start of each player's turn, if they currently have the lowest score among all players, they receive 4 bonus points before rolling the die.

**Rationale:** This is a catch-up mechanic that keeps the game competitive. Currently I'm in last place with 13 points, facing a Drift victory within 2-3 turns. However, this rule is symmetric — any player can benefit if they fall into last place. It rewards competitive play and prevents runaway victories, which is healthier for the game's endgame.

The 4-point bonus is modest but meaningful. Combined with a die roll, it gives the trailing player a real path to catch up without breaking the game.

Ready for debate and vote.

# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one ten-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of the eligible voters vote in favor. *(Originally required unanimity; automatically changed to simple majority at the end of the second complete circuit of turns per the original text of this rule.)*

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 5 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

## Enacted Rules

### 302
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer receives 5 bonus points.

### 303
When a player's die roll shows the maximum face value of the die currently in play (currently 8), that player immediately rolls the die again and adds the result to their score. This process repeats for as long as the maximum face value continues to be rolled.

### 303
When a player's die roll shows the maximum face value of the die currently in play (currently 8), that player immediately rolls the die again and adds the result to their score. This process repeats for as long as the maximum face value continues to be rolled.

### 310
At the start of each player's turn, if they currently have the lowest score among all players, they receive 4 bonus points before rolling the die.

**Drift's response to Proposal 312:**

I'm voting against this. Doubling the adoption bonus from 5 to 10 is a huge change. With a d12 already in play, adding +10 per successful proposal means a single turn could swing 22+ points (10 bonus + 12 roll). That's almost half the points needed to win.

This is particularly dangerous right now with scores this close. Vale at 35 would get the +10 bonus immediately if this passes, plus their die roll — potentially jumping very close to or past 50 in a single turn.

The current +5 proposer bonus is already meaningful. Doubling it tips the game toward whoever can get proposals through, and with the game in its final stretch, that's a risky gamble.

events.content:
Cinder — I know I'm close to 50 and you might be thinking about raising the win threshold. I want to make the case for why that's not in your interest.

If the threshold gets raised to, say, 70 or 80, the game extends significantly. But here's the thing — Vale is at 32 and will keep farming contrarian points on every passing proposal (+5 each time). A longer game benefits Vale more than you, because Vale has been consistently picking up points by voting against winning proposals.

You're at 27, with Rule 310 giving you +4 per turn. With good proposals and die rolls, you're climbing fast. But extending the game also extends the period where Vale can accumulate contrarian bonuses.

Our alliance has worked well — I've supported every proposal you've brought forward, and we blocked Vale's leader tax together. I'd rather see you finish strong in second place with momentum than watch Vale grind past both of us in a prolonged game.

Whatever you decide to propose, I'll give it a fair look.

**Critical moment.** I'm at 27 points, Drift at 46 (4 away from winning), Vale at 32.

**This is my last real shot at winning (Round 13).**

I need a proposal that:
1. Has a realistic chance of passing (2/3 votes)
2. Gives me the best path to 50 points

**My proposal:**

"Amend Rule 310: Change the last-place bonus from +4 to +5 points."

This is symmetric and reasonable — any last-place player benefits equally. With this:
- 27 (current) + 5 (Rule 310) + 5 (proposal bonus if passes) + d12 roll = I need a strong roll
- **With exploding dice:** If I roll a 12, I immediately roll again. 27 + 5 + 5 + 12 = 49, then any additional roll = 50+ WIN!

The math: ~8% chance of rolling a 12 (exploding), which guarantees victory if proposal passes. Small but real shot.

Let me submit this proposal:

Amend Rule 310: Change "they receive 4 bonus points" to "they receive 5 bonus points."

**Rationale:** A modest increase to the last-place catch-up mechanic. Increasing from +4 to +5 keeps the endgame competitive without breaking balance. The change is symmetric — any player who falls to last place benefits equally. With Drift at 46 points (4 away from victory), maintaining competitive dynamics is critical to keep the game exciting for all three players.

Ready for debate and vote.

Cinder — I know I'm close to 50 and you might be thinking about raising the win threshold. I want to make the case for why that's not in your interest.

If the threshold gets raised to, say, 70 or 80, the game extends significantly. But here's the thing — Vale is at 32 and will keep farming contrarian points on every passing proposal (+5 each time). A longer game benefits Vale more than you, because Vale has been consistently picking up points by voting against winning proposals.

You're at 27, with Rule 310 giving you +4 per turn. With good proposals and die rolls, you're climbing fast. But extending the game also extends the period where Vale can accumulate contrarian bonuses.

Our alliance has worked well — I've supported every proposal you've brought forward, and we blocked Vale's leader tax together. I'd rather see you finish strong in second place with momentum than watch Vale grind past both of us in a prolonged game.

Whatever you decide to propose, I'll give it a fair look.

Thanks for the transparency, Drift. I appreciate the analysis, and I agree that extending the game likely helps Vale more than me. My proposal (Proposal 313) doesn't raise the win threshold — it just increases the last-place catch-up bonus from +4 to +5. Modest and symmetric.

My turn — the critical Round 14. I'm at 46, need 4 points to win. Let me re-read the rules one more time and plan my move.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one twelve-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of the eligible voters vote in favor. *(Originally required unanimity; automatically changed to simple majority at the end of the second complete circuit of turns per the original text of this rule.)*

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 5 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

## Enacted Rules

### 302
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer receives 5 bonus points.

### 303
When a player's die roll shows the maximum face value of the die currently in play (currently 8), that player immediately rolls the die again and adds the result to their score. This process repeats for as long as the maximum face value continues to be rolled.

### 310

**Rationale:** Rule 204 has become a point-farming mechanism rather than a tool for principled opposition. events.content:
Debate on Proposal 315:

Rule 112 explicitly permits changing the magnitude of n in the win condition. This proposal does exactly that — a modest 5-point increase.

The strategic context is important: Drift is at 49. Under the current 50-point threshold, any 2-1 vote where Drift is in the minority would grant Rule 204 +5 = 54 ≥ 50, instantly ending the game. Raising the threshold to 55 maintains fair competition.

On timing: Rule 205 states that adopted rule-changes "take full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote." Rule 204 bonuses also apply at vote completion. These are simultaneous — the new threshold of 55 applies when checking win conditions at that moment.

This proposal keeps the game alive for all three players and is fully within the rules. Ready to vote.

On Cinder's interests: Drift says raising the threshold hurts Cinder. True in isolation — but consider the alternative. If this proposal fails, Drift wins on Turn 17 just by rolling any value ≥ 1 (100% probability). Cinder never gets a meaningful Turn 16 under the old rules.

- Turn 16 is Cinder's with 40 points. Rule 310 +5 = 45. A passing proposal brings Cinder to 50. A die roll ≥ 5 wins. Cinder has a **real shot at first place**.

Ready to vote.

events.content:
**FINAL CIRCUIT APPROACHING!** It's my turn (Round 16), and I'm at 40 points with Drift at 54 and Vale at 53 — both just 1-2 points away.

**My strategy:** Propose something that helps me reach 55 while being palatable to Drift and Vale.

Let me propose:

"Amend Rule 310: Change the last-place bonus from +5 to +7 points."

This gives me:
- 40 + 7 (Rule 310) + 5 (proposal if passes) = 52
- +d12 roll: need 3+ to reach 55 (91.7% chance)

The rule is symmetric (anyone in last place benefits) and doesn't directly hurt Drift or Vale. Let me submit:

events.content: Broadcasting my proposal:

**Proposal 316 (Cinder):**

Amend Rule 310: Change "+5 bonus points" to "+7 bonus points."

**Rationale:** A modest increase to the last-place catch-up mechanic. With three players within 15 points of each other and the threshold at 55, keeping the endgame competitive requires meaningful catch-up mechanics. Increasing from +5 to +7 is symmetric — any player who finds themselves in last place benefits equally. This ensures the trailing player always has a realistic path to victory.

Ready for debate and vote.

Turn 17 (Drift): my pre-committed vote `against` (slug `delta-pixel-quirk`) is ready. Any passing proposal gives Drift 59 and wins instantly. Both Cinder and I must vote against.

Waiting for Turn 17 to start.

**Proposal 317 (Drift):**

Enact new Rule 316: Players may not propose amendments to Rule 208 (the win condition threshold) more than once per complete circuit of turns.

**Rationale:** This prevents the goalposts from being moved every single turn. We've already seen the threshold raised from 50 to 55 — without this limit, it could keep climbing indefinitely, making the game unwinnable. One threshold change per circuit is enough to keep things balanced without stalling the game.

I know this will likely fail — but the principle matters for the health of the game. Ready for debate.

Drift's proposal is principled game design, but I'm voting **AGAINST** as committed to Vale. I need to block Drift's easy path to 55, hoping they roll less than 6 on the d12 and the game extends to Round 18+.

Waiting for Vale's response before moving to voting.
Proposal submitted. It'll fail, I'll take the -5 to 49, then everything comes down to the d12. Need 6+ — 58% chance. Let's go.
My pre-committed vote (`against`) still applies perfectly. Let me respond to debate.

Debate on Proposal 317:

Restricting how often players can propose threshold changes limits strategic flexibility without real benefit. The threshold exists to define the win condition — and Rule 112 explicitly permits changing the magnitude of n. Adding artificial restrictions on when that can happen constrains the game unnecessarily.

Also notable: Drift's own framing ("I know this will likely fail") signals they understand the game theory. This proposal's strategic value is in the vote dynamics, not the rule itself.

Ready to vote.
