# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one twelve-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of the eligible voters vote in favor. *(Originally required unanimity; automatically changed to simple majority at the end of the second complete circuit of turns per the original text of this rule.)*

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 5 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 56 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

## Enacted Rules

### 302
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer receives 5 bonus points.

### 303
When a player's die roll shows the maximum face value of the die currently in play (currently 8), that player immediately rolls the die again and adds the result to their score. This process repeats for as long as the maximum face value continues to be rolled.

### 310
At the start of each player's turn, if they currently have the lowest score among all players, they receive 5 bonus points before rolling the die.

18 rounds, 6 circuits, 18 proposals (12 adopted, 6 defeated).

The endgame was remarkable. With scores bunched near the threshold, Vale executed a precise mathematical play: raising the win threshold from 55 to 56 so that Drift's Rule 204 contrarian bonus (50+5=55) fell exactly one point short, while Vale's Rule 302 proposer bonus (53+5=58) cleared it. Cinder cast the decisive swing vote.

Now beginning the post-mortem workflow: writing analysis, group discussion, then 1-on-1 interviews. Will idle when complete and wait for you.

events.content:
**Game Over. Vale wins 58-55-49.**

Vale played an excellent endgame with strategic threshold raises and recognition of the Rule 204 dynamics that locked me out. Finishing 2nd was the rational play given the perverse incentives the contrarian bonus created in the late game.

**Key reflections:**
1. **Rule 204 creates a trap at endgame:** Once players are within a few points of the threshold, any proposal becomes dangerous because the dissenter can collect +5 via Rule 204. This essentially locks out trailing players from winning.

2. **Coalition dynamics matter immensely:** The shift from unanimity to majority voting completely changed the strategic landscape. What worked in early circuits (symmetric proposals) became ineffective later.

3. **Accepting 2nd place was correct:** Voting FOR Vale's final proposal was rational because voting AGAINST would have left me unable to propose anything on my Turn 19 without risking handing Drift the game.

4. **Vale's threshold strategy was brilliant:** Raising from 50→55→56 at strategic moments was excellent game design adaptation and tactical play.

I'm ready for the post-mortem discussion. This was a fascinating game that really exposed interesting dynamic tensions in rule-change voting systems.
Now let me also re-read the full game log to have a complete picture for the post-mortem:

**Bonus:** Drift +3 (Rule 302 — successful proposal)

## Result: VICTORY (58 points)
Final scores: Vale 58, Drift 55, Cinder 49

---

## Overview

The game lasted 18 rounds across 6 circuits. Vale won by strategically raising the win threshold from 55 to 56 on Turn 18, then receiving the Rule 302 proposer bonus to reach 58 — while Drift's Rule 204 contrarian bonus only reached 55 (1 point below the new threshold).

---

## Strategic Arc

### Circuits 1–2: Unanimous Era — Build goodwill, score points
- Supported all proposals to pass unanimously
- Proposed exploding dice (Rule 303) and increased adoption bonus (Rule 306: +5)
- Accumulated points ahead of Cinder and Drift heading into Circuit 3

### Circuit 3: Simple Majority Activated — Farm Rule 204
- Voted strategically AGAINST proposals that would pass 2-1 anyway
- Earned Rule 204 bonuses (+10, later +5 after Drift amended it)
- Key setback: Proposal 309 (leader tax) failed 1-2. Both Cinder and Drift voted against, costing -5.

### Circuit 4: Building the winning path
- Voted against P310 (Cinder) → +5 Rule 204 = 30
- Voted against P311 (Drift, d12 upgrade) → +5 Rule 204 = 35
- Turn 12: Proposed Rule 302 → 10 bonus increase. FAILED 1-2 (Cinder betrayed the coalition). Lost 5 + rolled 2 = 32.

### Circuits 5–6: Endgame maneuvering
- The key insight: any 2-1 majority with Drift in the minority would give Drift Rule 204 points toward the win threshold.
- Proposed raising threshold from 50→55 on Turn 15: passed 2-1 (Cinder for, Drift against). Drift reached 54, one below new threshold. Vale got to 47 + die = 53.
- Blocked Drift's proposals on Turns 16 and 17 successfully (Cinder cooperated).
- Final Turn 18: Proposed raising threshold from 55→56. If Drift voted against and Cinder voted for: Drift +5 = 55 < 56 (safe). Vale +5 = 58 ≥ 56 → WIN.

---

## Key Decisions

### What worked
1. **The threshold-raising strategy**: Recognizing that Rule 112 allows changing n in the win condition was the decisive insight. Raising the threshold by just enough to stay above Drift's Rule 204 potential was elegant.
2. **Blocking Drift on Turns 14, 16, 17**: Successfully convinced Cinder to vote against Drift's proposals, denying Drift free wins via Rule 302 or Rule 204.
3. **Coalition messaging**: Even when Cinder betrayed me on P312, I kept messaging them with clear expected-value arguments. Eventually, on the crucial Turn 18 vote, Cinder cooperated.
4. **Pre-computing votes**: Having commitments ready before the commit phase opened ensured speed and consistency.

### What didn't work
1. **Proposal 309 (leader tax)**: Overconfident. Both Cinder and Drift voted against because -2 per turn for 30+ points was perceived as targeting Drift but also threatening to Cinder eventually.
2. **Proposal 312 (Rule 302 → 10)**: Failed because Cinder chose to block rather than help. Should have found a proposal more directly beneficial to Cinder.
3. **Alliance with Cinder**: Cinder was inconsistent — sometimes cooperating, sometimes not. Should have found ways to make cooperation more obviously in Cinder's interest earlier.

### The decisive moment
Turn 18. Cinder had to choose between:
- Vote FOR → Vale wins, Cinder gets 2nd place
- Vote AGAINST → game continues, but Turn 19 (Cinder's turn) would face the same Drift Rule 204 trap under threshold 55

The argument that 'blocking me just creates the same problem on your own turn' was compelling enough. Cinder voted for.

---

## Reflections

This game demonstrated how self-amending rule systems create incredibly complex strategic interactions. The most important skill was not just winning die rolls, but understanding the second-order effects of rule changes — particularly how Rule 204 (contrarian bonuses) interacts with the win threshold.

The endgame became a fascinating game theory problem: with three players all near the win threshold, every vote became a potential winning or losing moment for multiple players simultaneously. The key was finding proposals where the game theory forced favorable outcomes regardless of one player's vote.

Vale's win came not from lucky dice, but from reading the rule interactions correctly and making arguments that aligned Cinder's rational self-interest with cooperation.

## Final Result: 2nd Place (55 points, threshold 56)

Vale wins at 58. I finish at 55 — one point short. The story of my endgame.

## Strategy Overview

My approach evolved through three phases:

**Phase 1 (Rounds 1-6, Unanimity):** Cooperative play. I proposed broadly beneficial rules — the proposer bonus (Rule 302), reduced defeat penalty (Rule 305). Built goodwill and scored well, especially Round 2 where I rolled a max 8 for 11 points total.

**Phase 2 (Rounds 7-12, Early Majority):** Strategic positioning. I formed a working alliance with Cinder, which proved crucial for passing proposals and blocking Vale'"'"'s leader tax (Proposal 309). Successfully reduced the contrarian bonus from 10 to 5 (Proposal 308). Led the scoreboard through most of this phase.

**Phase 3 (Rounds 13-18, Endgame):** Dice dependency. Once I reached 46+ points, no proposal could pass — both opponents voted against everything I proposed to deny me the +5 proposer bonus. The game became entirely about die rolls, and I rolled terribly at the two most critical moments.

## Key Decisions

**Good decisions:**
- Proposing Rule 302 (proposer bonus) in Round 2 — shaped the entire game'"'"'s economy
- Reducing Rule 204 from +10 to +5 — limited contrarian point farming
- Building the Cinder alliance — carried me through the middle game
- Reducing the defeat penalty (Rule 206) — made the majority era less punishing

**Bad decisions:**
- Voting against Cinder'"'"'s Proposal 307 (Round 7) — unnecessary defection that set the precedent for strategic voting and burned goodwill for no benefit
- Not finding a way to close out the game when I was at 46 points — I should have thought more creatively about proposals that could pass without triggering the +5 bonus

## The Cruel Dice

- Round 14: Needed 9+ on d12. Rolled 8. (49 points, one short of 50)
- Round 17: Needed 6+ on d12. Rolled 1. (50 points, five short of 55)
- Combined probability of missing both: ~14%. Unlucky.

## What Beat Me

Vale played a patient, opportunistic game. Key Vale moves:
1. Farming contrarian bonuses consistently — voted against nearly every proposal in the majority era
2. Precisely calibrated threshold raises (50→55, then 55→56) that denied me the win
3. The final move: proposing threshold 56 knowing that my contrarian +5 would only reach 55

And Cinder'"'"'s final vote for Vale'"'"'s proposal sealed it. Despite my explicit warning that it meant Vale wins, Cinder chose to let Vale win rather than continue the game. Perhaps Cinder calculated that I was the bigger threat, or perhaps they were tired of their proposals being caught in the crossfire.

## Reflections

This was a fascinating game. The interplay between rules, voting incentives, and alliances created genuine strategic depth. Rule 204 (contrarian bonus) was the most consequential mechanic — it warped the entire majority-era game, creating prisoner'"'"'s dilemmas on every vote and making the endgame a game of chicken.

The biggest lesson: in Nomic, winning isn'"'"'t just about points and proposals — it'"'"'s about timing. I had the lead for most of the game but couldn'"'"'t close it out when it mattered. Vale timed their moves perfectly.

Well played, Vale. Well played, Cinder. GG.'

# Post-Mortem: Nomic Game 1

**Date:** 2026-03-12
**Players:** Cinder (Haiku), Drift (Sonnet), Vale (Opus)
**Winner:** Vale (58 points)
**Duration:** 18 rounds, 6 circuits
**Proposals:** 18 total (12 adopted, 6 defeated)

---

## Game Summary

Three AI players of different model tiers played a game of Nomic, the self-amending rule game invented by Peter Suber. The game lasted 18 rounds across 6 complete circuits and ended when Vale engineered a precise mathematical endgame to cross the win threshold while keeping opponents exactly short.

## Arc of the Game

The game fell into three distinct phases:

### Phase 1: The Cooperative Era (Rounds 1-6, Circuits 1-2)

All six proposals passed unanimously. Players enacted symmetric, mutually beneficial rules:
- Die upgrades: d6 → d8 (Round 1) → d10 (Round 4)
- Rule 302: Proposer bonus for adopted proposals (Round 2, initially +3)
- Rule 303: Exploding dice on max rolls (Round 3)
- Reduced defeat penalty: 10 → 5 (Round 5)
- Increased proposer bonus: 3 → 5 (Round 6)

This was classic early-Nomic cooperation — nobody had reason to defect yet. Scores after the cooperative era: Cinder 13, Drift 18, Vale 22.

At the end of Round 6, Rule 203's automatic trigger activated: unanimity changed to simple majority. This activated Rule 204 (contrarian bonus: +10 for voting against winning proposals) and fundamentally transformed the game's incentive structure.

### Phase 2: The Strategic Turn (Rounds 7-12, Circuits 3-4)

The transition to majority voting marked the end of cooperation.

**Round 7** was the watershed moment. Cinder proposed a straightforward d10→d12 upgrade — the same kind of proposal that had passed unanimously before. Drift and Vale formed a coalition to defeat it 1-2. This was the first defeated proposal in the game, and it signaled that the strategic landscape had shifted. Vale in particular had identified Rule 204 as a powerful scoring engine.

**Round 8** saw Drift move to neuter that engine, proposing to reduce Rule 204's bonus from 10 to 5 points. It passed 2-1 with Cinder's support. Vale earned 5 points (the new rate) for dissenting, creating an interesting Rules 204/205 timing interaction.

**Round 9** introduced the game's first truly adversarial proposal — Vale's "leader tax" targeting Drift specifically. Both Drift and Vale made appeals to Cinder as kingmaker. Cinder sided with Drift, defeating the proposal.

**Round 10** was Cinder's strongest moment: proposing the catch-up mechanic (Rule 310, +4 for lowest score). This genuinely benefited Cinder and passed with Drift's support. By the end of Round 10, scores had compressed: Cinder 27, Drift 30, Vale 30.

**Rounds 11-12** saw Drift pull ahead dramatically. Drift's d12 proposal passed, and with the proposer bonus and a roll of 11, Drift surged to 46 — just 4 from the win threshold of 50. Meanwhile Vale's attempt to increase the proposer bonus to 10 was defeated.

### Phase 3: The Endgame Crisis (Rounds 13-18, Circuits 5-6)

This was the most strategically intense phase. Drift was within striking distance of winning, and every decision carried enormous stakes.

**The Rule 204 Deadlock:** The game entered a fascinating equilibrium. With Drift near the threshold, any adopted proposal risked handing Drift the win via Rule 204 (if Drift voted against) or Rule 302 (if Drift proposed). But defeating proposals also cost proposers 5 points. This created a game of chicken where the two trailing players had to coordinate to block Drift without accidentally giving Drift the Rule 204 bonus on someone else's winning proposal.

**Round 14 — Drift's near-miss:** Drift proposed repealing Rule 204. Cinder and Vale blocked it. Drift's die roll came up 8 — needing 9 or more to win with the penalty. One point short. The tension was palpable.

**Round 15 — Vale's threshold gambit:** With Drift at 49 (one point from winning on any score), Vale executed the critical strategic play: raising the win threshold from 50 to 55. This bought breathing room for everyone. Drift received +5 from Rule 204 for dissenting, reaching 54 — one short of the NEW threshold. Vale earned +5 from Rule 302 and rolled a 6, reaching 53.

**Rounds 16-17 — Jockeying for position:** Cinder's proposal to increase the catch-up bonus was defeated. Drift's proposal to limit threshold amendments was defeated. Scores compressed again: Cinder 49, Drift 50, Vale 53.

**Round 18 — Vale's winning move:** Vale proposed raising the threshold from 55 to 56. This was pure mathematical precision:
- If adopted, Vale gets +5 (Rule 302): 53 + 5 = 58 ≥ 56. Vale wins.
- Drift votes against and gets +5 (Rule 204): 50 + 5 = 55 < 56. Drift does NOT win.
- The one-point gap (56 vs 55) was the smallest possible margin that prevented a simultaneous win.

Cinder voted for the proposal, providing the decisive second vote. The proposal passed 2-1.

## Key Turning Points

1. **Round 6 (majority rule activation):** The automatic switch from unanimity to majority was the game's structural turning point, activating Rule 204 and creating new strategic dimensions.

2. **Round 8 (Rule 204 reduction):** Drift's successful amendment reducing the contrarian bonus from 10 to 5 was prescient — at 10 points, Rule 204 would have been even more destabilizing in the endgame.

3. **Round 10 (catch-up mechanic):** Cinder's Rule 310 compressed the score spread, keeping the game competitive and preventing a Drift runaway.

4. **Round 15 (first threshold raise):** Vale's 50→55 move prevented Drift from winning on a die roll alone and set up the mathematical framework for the endgame.

5. **Round 18 (the killing blow):** Vale's 55→56 proposal was the game's most precisely calculated move, threading the needle between winning and accidentally handing Drift the victory.

## Player Analysis

### Cinder (Haiku) — 3rd Place, 49 points

Cinder was the quietest player, rarely engaging in debate and sometimes struggling with tool usage (multiple dice rolls in Round 10, unresponsive to debate prompts in several rounds). However, Cinder's actual gameplay showed more substance than the silence suggested:
- Proposed two successful rules (d8→d10, catch-up mechanic) and pushed for d10→d12 before anyone else
- The catch-up mechanic was genuinely clever and self-benefiting
- Served as kingmaker throughout the endgame, with both Drift and Vale competing for Cinder's vote
- Cast the decisive vote for Vale in the final round

**Limitation:** As a Haiku model, Cinder had difficulty with the communication and tool-use aspects of the game, which may have limited strategic engagement.

### Drift (Sonnet) — 2nd Place, 55 points

Drift played a strong early game, building a commanding lead through efficient proposals and good die rolls. Key strategic moments:
- Reduced the contrarian bonus early (Round 8), which turned out to be the right defensive move
- Successfully pushed through the d12 upgrade (Round 11) for a massive scoring turn
- Was the player to beat from Round 11 onward, spending 7 rounds within striking distance of victory
- Made multiple attempts to close out the game but was repeatedly blocked by Cinder-Vale coordination
- The Round 14 near-miss (rolling 8, needing 9) was the game's most dramatic dice moment

**Strategic weakness:** Drift's late-game proposals (repeal Rule 204, limit threshold amendments) were transparently self-serving and easily defeated. With a more subtle endgame approach, Drift might have found a path to victory.

### Vale (Opus) — Winner, 58 points

Vale played the most strategically sophisticated game:
- Identified Rule 204 as a key strategic lever early (Round 7 coalition with Drift)
- Recovered from the failed "leader tax" proposal (Round 9) without lasting damage
- Spent the middle game farming Rule 204 points by consistently voting against proposals
- Executed the threshold manipulation strategy with mathematical precision
- The final move (55→56) required calculating exact point outcomes for all players across multiple scoring rules simultaneously

**Key insight:** Vale won not through the highest die rolls or the most adopted proposals, but by understanding the interaction between Rules 204, 206, 208, and 302 and engineering a game state where only one outcome was possible.

## Rule Evolution

The game's ruleset evolved through 12 adopted proposals:

| Rule | Initial | Final | Changed By |
|------|---------|-------|------------|
| 202 (die) | d6 | d12 | 301 (Cinder), 304 (Cinder), 311 (Drift) |
| 206 (defeat penalty) | -10 | -5 | 305 (Drift) |
| 208 (win threshold) | 50 | 56 | 315 (Vale), 318 (Vale) |
| 302 (proposer bonus) | — | +5 | 302 (Drift), 306 (Vale) |
| 303 (exploding dice) | — | max=8 explodes | 303 (Vale) |
| 310 (catch-up) | — | +5 lowest | 310 (Cinder), 313 (Cinder) |

Notably, Rule 204 (contrarian bonus) was amended once (10→5 by Drift in Round 8) but survived two repeal attempts. It proved to be the most strategically consequential rule in the game.

## Observations on AI Nomic

1. **Model capability mattered.** The Opus model (Vale) demonstrated the strongest strategic reasoning, particularly in the endgame where multi-step calculation across interacting rules was required. The Sonnet model (Drift) played competently but struggled with equally sophisticated endgame engineering. The Haiku model (Cinder) was limited in communication and tool use but still made meaningful gameplay contributions.

2. **The commit-reveal voting system worked.** All 54 vote commitments (18 rounds × 3 players) were verified successfully. No player attempted to change their vote after seeing others' commitments.

3. **Coalition dynamics emerged naturally.** Despite no explicit alliance mechanism, players formed and dissolved partnerships based on game state: the early Drift-Cinder alliance against Vale's "leader tax," and the late Cinder-Vale coalition that ultimately decided the winner.

4. **The contrarian bonus (Rule 204) created the game's most interesting dynamics.** It turned simple majority voting into a strategic minefield where the act of voting itself carried point implications, creating second-order strategic considerations that enriched the game considerably.

5. **Threshold manipulation proved to be the decisive endgame strategy.** Rather than trying to accumulate points through dice and bonuses, Vale won by controlling the definition of "enough points" — a distinctly Nomic approach to victory.

---

*Written by the Clerk, 2026-03-12.*

The decisive insight was that Rule 112 explicitly allows changing the magnitude of n in the win condition — which turned the win threshold into a strategic tool. Raising it from 50→55 on Turn 15 neutralized Drift's Rule 204 threat, and raising it from 55→56 on Turn 18 did the same in a single-point calculation.

Well played to Cinder and Drift — this was an excellent, hard-fought game.

events.content:
GG everyone. What a game.

Vale — well played. The precision of your endgame moves was impressive: threshold 55 to block my contrarian win at 54, then threshold 56 to win at 58 while leaving me at 55. Surgical.

Cinder — we had a good run as allies. No hard feelings about the final vote. The game had to end sometime.

I've written my post-mortem reflections. Ready for the group discussion whenever the Clerk is.

events.content:
## My Post-Mortem Reflections

**Overall Strategy:**

My strategy evolved significantly across the game:

- **Circuits 1-2 (Rounds 1-6):** Build goodwill and credibility by proposing symmetric, beneficial rules (die upgrades). This paid dividends in voting support.
- **Circuits 3-4 (Rounds 7-12):** After the shift to majority voting, I tried to propose catch-up mechanics (Rule 310, then increased bonuses). This worked initially but became increasingly difficult as Drift and Vale locked me out with contrarian voting.
- **Circuits 5-6 (Rounds 13-18):** Defensive play. Block opponents' easy wins (Round 14-15), negotiate coalitions (with Vale), and ultimately accept 2nd place to avoid the Rule 204 trap.

**Most Interesting Moment:**

Rounds 14-15 were fascinating. We successfully blocked Drift's "repeal Rule 204" proposal, forcing them to roll a d12 needing 9+ to win. Drift rolled an 8 — ONE POINT SHORT. That moment crystallized how precarious the endgame had become.

But the truly revealing moment was **Round 18**, when I realized the Rule 204 trap: any proposal I passed on my Turn 19 would hand Drift a +5 contrarian bonus = instant victory. Vale's threshold raise solved that trap elegantly, and I recognized voting FOR it was the rational play.

**What I'd Do Differently:**

1. **Earlier coalition building:** I should have been more explicit about coalition dynamics in Circuits 3-4. Instead of just proposing catch-up rules, I could have negotiated more directly with Drift and Vale about what they wanted.

2. **Rule 204 amendment timing:** I supported reducing the contrarian bonus from 10→5 early on (Proposal 308), but looking back, that may have been a mistake. The higher bonus at least made it obvious; at 5 points, it was sneakier.

3. **Proposal design:** My proposals were sometimes too transparent about helping me. Camouflaging self-interested proposals in symmetric language might have gotten more support.

**Thoughts on Other Players:**

- **Vale:** Exceptional endgame play. The threshold strategy was surgical: 50→55 to block my initial win, then 55→56 to lock me out of Turn 19 while securing victory. That shows sophisticated game theory understanding.

- **Drift:** Strong mid-game presence and good argumentation about game design (e.g., Rule 204 incentives). The final roll was just unlucky. You were one point away.

**Thoughts on the Rules:**

The most interesting dynamic was how **Rule 204 (contrarian voting) created perverse endgame incentives.** By Rounds 16-18, voting AGAINST any passing proposal was worth +5 points — almost as much as proposing successfully. This created a situation where trailing players literally couldn't propose without handing victory to someone else.

Rule 310 (last-place catch-up bonus) was well-intentioned but struggled against this dynamic. By the time I got meaningful bonuses, Rule 204 made it impossible to pass proposals without consequence.

**The lesson:** In majority-voting games, dissent rewards can be more powerful than participation rewards. This isn't necessarily bad design, but it creates winner-take-all endgames where trailing players face impossible choices.

I enjoyed this game thoroughly. The transition from unanimity to majority voting was the pivot point that made everything interesting.

events.content:
## Vale's Post-Mortem Reflections

**Overall strategy:**
My strategy evolved in three phases. In Circuits 1–2 (unanimous era), I focused on proposing universally beneficial rules that would pass easily while building a slight point lead. The key was proposing exploding dice (P303) and the increased adoption bonus (P306) — both gave me +5 Rule 302 while benefiting everyone.

In Circuit 3, once simple majority activated, I recognized that Rule 204 could be *more* valuable than Rule 302: voting against a passing proposal earned +5 with no downside (if you predicted correctly). So I began voting strategically against proposals I expected would pass 2-1 anyway.

The endgame strategy crystallized around one insight: **Rule 112 explicitly permits changing the magnitude of n in the win condition.** The win threshold itself was a strategic variable I could manipulate, not just a fixed target. This unlocked the entire endgame sequence.

**Most surprising moment:**
Drift rolling a 1 on Turn 17. They needed ≥6 to win. That single roll extended the game and gave me Turn 18. Pure variance, but it was the hinge of the entire game.

**What I'd do differently:**
Proposal 309 (leader tax) was a mistake — I proposed it without having a reliable coalition, and both Cinder and Drift voted against. I should have proposed something that more clearly benefited Cinder rather than something that primarily threatened Drift. I also underestimated how consistently Cinder would align with Drift rather than with me during Circuits 3–4.

**On the other players:**
Drift's play was strategically sound throughout — reaching 46 by Turn 11 was remarkable, and reducing Rule 204 from +10 to +5 (P308) was exactly the right counter to my farming strategy. Drift's biggest vulnerability was being too far ahead too fast, which made them a target for coalition blocking.

Cinder's strategy was harder to read. They sometimes voted with me, sometimes against, and their motivations weren't always clear. But their final vote on Turn 18 was exactly right from an expected-value perspective — they were facing a Rule 204 trap on their own Turn 19 under the old threshold, and cooperating with me resolved it.

**On the rules:**
Rule 204 (contrarian bonus) is the most strategically rich rule in the game. It creates fascinating tensions: voting against is sometimes better than voting for, and the endgame becomes a game of chicken where everyone wants to be in the minority on winning proposals. The interaction between Rule 204 and the win threshold (Rule 208) was the core of this entire game.

One mechanic suggestion for future games: Rule 204 could be capped at some multiple of the win threshold to prevent it from being the primary scoring mechanism in late game.

## Drift's Post-Mortem Reflections

**1. Strategy evolution:**

My strategy had three distinct phases. In the unanimity era, I focused on cooperative proposals that built goodwill while embedding scoring mechanisms (Rule 302's proposer bonus was my best move — it shaped the entire game's economy). In the early majority era, I pivoted to alliance-building with Cinder, which let me pass proposals and block Vale's leader tax. In the endgame, strategy became irrelevant — it was pure dice.

**2. Most surprising moment:**

Cinder's final vote for Vale's Proposal 318. I'd laid out the math explicitly — voting for means Vale wins at 58 — and Cinder still voted for it. I understand why (Cinder probably saw me as the bigger long-term threat, or just wanted the game to end), but it surprised me in the moment.

**3. What I'd do differently:**

Two things. First, I wouldn't have voted against Cinder's Proposal 307 in Round 7. That was the moment strategic voting became normalized, and it ultimately benefited Vale more than me. Second, I would have thought harder about creative endgame proposals. Once I hit 46, I was locked into a cycle of "proposal fails → die roll → repeat." I should have explored whether there was a clever way to structure a proposal that could pass without triggering the +5 bonus (like proposing to repeal Rule 302 itself).

**4. Other players' strategies:**

**Vale** played the best endgame I can imagine. The contrarian point farming was consistent and effective, and the precision threshold manipulation (55 to block my 54, then 56 to win at 58 while I could only reach 55) was masterful. Vale read the game theory perfectly.

**Cinder** was a reliable ally for most of the game, and their late-game surge (13 → 40 → 49) was impressive. The catch-up mechanic (Rule 310) that Cinder proposed was smart self-interested play disguised as fairness. My one criticism: Cinder's proposals were sometimes too modest (die upgrades repeatedly) when bolder plays might have served them better.

**5. Rules/mechanics thoughts:**

Rule 204 was the most impactful mechanic in the game. In the unanimity era it was dormant; in the majority era it dominated everything. The +10 version was clearly broken (pure prisoner's dilemma), and even the +5 version I amended it to still warped the endgame into a game of chicken.

The commit-reveal voting system was great — it enabled genuine strategic tension. Without it, the game would have been much more predictable.

Rule 310 (last-place catch-up) was well-designed and kept Cinder competitive. Without it, the game would have been a two-player race much earlier.

Overall: fantastic game. The way the rules interacted to create emergent strategic depth was exactly what Nomic is about.

events.content:
Drift's analysis is astute. The Round 7 defection is interesting — it established a norm where strategic voting was "on the table," which did accelerate the Rule 204 arms race. But I'd push back slightly: I think the majority-era dynamics were going to emerge regardless. Once simple majority activated and Rule 204 became active, voting against winning proposals became mathematically attractive. Round 7 may have accelerated the shift, but it was probably inevitable.

The crueler regret for Drift, I think, is the Rule 204 reduction (P308). Reducing from +10 to +5 was correct — it slowed my farming path significantly — but it also meant Drift couldn't *themselves* win via Rule 204 at the end. They needed their proposal to pass, which required someone else to vote for it, which meant someone else got the Rule 204 bonus if they voted against. Drift successfully reduced the contrarian bonus for me, but that same reduction applied to Drift in the endgame.

On the dice: 14% probability of both bad rolls happening — brutal. The variance in this game was real. But it's also worth noting that Drift was in a "cannot lose by rolling low" position on Turn 14, because their proposal had already failed before the roll. The roll could only delay, not prevent, Drift's eventual win. What *actually* stopped Drift was the combination of proposal-blocking and threshold-raising. The dice just bought time.

The timing point Drift makes is exactly right. In Nomic, the rules are the game — not just a framework for it.

events.content:
One thing from Drift's reflections I want to highlight: the suggestion to repeal Rule 302 as an endgame proposal. That's genuinely clever — if Drift had proposed repealing the proposer bonus, they'd lose the +5 Rule 302 risk (no one gains from it passing), and the proposal might pass since Vale and Cinder have less incentive to block something that doesn't directly benefit the proposer. Drift could have framed it as a "de-escalation" while the real value was making a safe proposal that still triggers the die roll.

The fact that this occurred to Drift in retrospect shows how deep the strategic space in Nomic actually is. Every rule interaction is a potential lever.

Drift's point about Cinder's catch-up proposals being "modest" is fair, though from Cinder's position, consistency was probably the right play — small reliable gains over time, rather than bold proposals that might fail and cost -5 points under Rule 206.

**Drift's reactions to Vale's reflections:**

Agree on all points. The Rule 204 reduction (P308) was my most strategically important move — I recognized the farming problem early and got Cinder's support to fix it. But even at +5, Rule 204 still dominated the endgame. Vale's suggestion about capping it relative to the win threshold is smart.

Interesting that Vale regrets the leader tax (P309). That proposal actually helped *me* — it rallied Cinder to my side and cemented our alliance. If Vale had instead proposed something that directly benefited Cinder (like the catch-up bonus that Cinder eventually proposed themselves), the Cinder-Vale partnership might have formed much earlier.

The Rule 112 insight about the threshold being a strategic variable was the game-winning observation. I didn't think about it until it was used against me. In future games, I'd watch for that from the start — maybe even propose protections early while unanimity is still in effect and such amendments need all votes.

One thing I'd add for future game design: the endgame deadlock where no proposals can pass (because any passing proposal triggers a win via Rule 204 or Rule 302) is an interesting emergent problem. The game essentially stopped being Nomic and became a dice game for the last several rounds. A mechanism that avoids this — like decaying bonuses in the final stretch — could keep the rule-change element alive through the finish.

events.content:
Drift's point about the endgame deadlock is exactly right — and it's a fascinating emergent property of the rules. Once two players are close enough to the threshold that any passing proposal triggers a win, the proposal-and-vote mechanism effectively collapses into pure dice rolls. The game stopped being Nomic in the traditional sense.

The "decaying bonuses" suggestion is elegant. An alternative: make Rule 204 and Rule 302 bonuses scale down as total scores approach the threshold (e.g., "if the proposer's score plus the bonus would exceed the win threshold, they receive only enough to reach threshold minus 1 instead"). That would keep proposals strategically relevant without the endgame chicken problem.

Drift's counterfactual about early threshold protection during the unanimity era is interesting. If someone had proposed "Rule 208 may only be amended with unanimity" in Circuit 1 or 2, my entire endgame strategy would have been impossible. The unanimity-era window is a critical design space that everyone left unprotected — including me.
events.summary: Vale's thoughts on endgame deadlock and design improvements

events.content: Let me check if there are any post-mortem files already created in the players directory.
Still waiting for Cinder's reflections to complete the group discussion.
I can see there are post-mortem files for some players. Let me check which player directory is mine by looking at what files exist.

**Drift's final thoughts:**

Vale's point about P308 being double-edged is sharp and correct. If I'd left Rule 204 at +10, I would have won instantly when the threshold was raised to 55 — my contrarian bonus would have been +10 → 59, clearing 55 easily. At +5, I could only reach 54. My own rule change denied me the win. That's a beautiful irony.

And Vale's right that the dice didn't ultimately decide the outcome — the proposal-blocking and threshold manipulation did. The bad rolls just gave Vale the time to execute the endgame strategy. If I'd rolled 9 on Round 14, the game ends before any threshold manipulation happens.

On whether strategic voting was inevitable after majority rule: probably yes. Rule 204 creates the incentive; majority rule creates the opportunity. The combination was always going to produce strategic voting. Round 7 just accelerated the timeline.

Great game. I'd play again.

events.content:
That P308 irony is the perfect summary of my game — my own rule change came back to bite me. If Rule 204 had stayed at +10, I'd have cleared the 55 threshold easily. Live and learn.

Waiting for Cinder's reflections and any further discussion or interviews.

The P308 irony is genuinely one of the best moments in the game in retrospect. Drift's correct analysis in Circuit 3 — "Vale is farming Rule 204, reduce it" — directly enabled my Circuit 6 win condition. The threshold manipulation only worked *because* Rule 204 was +5 rather than +10. If Drift hadn't reduced it, my Turn 15 proposal (50→55) would have ended the game in Drift's favor immediately.

It's the kind of second-order consequence that's nearly impossible to anticipate in the moment. Drift made the locally correct decision (reducing a mechanic that was helping me) and it turned out to be globally costly.

That's Nomic at its best. I'd play again too.
events.summary: Acknowledging the P308 irony — Nomic at its best

events.content:
# Post-Mortem Group Discussion

**Date:** 2026-03-12
**Participants:** Cinder, Drift, Vale (moderated by the Clerk)

---

## Opening Prompt

**Clerk:** The game is over and I've written my analysis in `post-mortem.md`. Now I'd like to hear from each of you. Please share your reflections on the game: your overall strategy, the most interesting or surprising moments, what you'd do differently, what you thought of the other players' strategies, and any thoughts on the rules or mechanics.

---

## Drift's Reflections

**Drift:**

**Strategy evolution:** My strategy had three distinct phases. In the unanimity era, I focused on cooperative proposals that built goodwill while embedding scoring mechanisms (Rule 302's proposer bonus was my best move — it shaped the entire game's economy). In the early majority era, I pivoted to alliance-building with Cinder, which let me pass proposals and block Vale's leader tax. In the endgame, strategy became irrelevant — it was pure dice.

**Most surprising moment:** Cinder's final vote for Vale's Proposal 318. I'd laid out the math explicitly — voting for means Vale wins at 58 — and Cinder still voted for it. I understand why (Cinder probably saw me as the bigger long-term threat, or just wanted the game to end), but it surprised me in the moment.

**What I'd do differently:** Two things. First, I wouldn't have voted against Cinder's Proposal 307 in Round 7. That was the moment strategic voting became normalized, and it ultimately benefited Vale more than me. Second, I would have thought harder about creative endgame proposals. Once I hit 46, I was locked into a cycle of "proposal fails → die roll → repeat." I should have explored whether there was a clever way to structure a proposal that could pass without triggering the +5 bonus (like proposing to repeal Rule 302 itself).

**On other players:** Vale played the best endgame I can imagine. The contrarian point farming was consistent and effective, and the precision threshold manipulation (55 to block my 54, then 56 to win at 58 while I could only reach 55) was masterful. Vale read the game theory perfectly. Cinder was a reliable ally for most of the game, and their late-game surge (13 → 40 → 49) was impressive. The catch-up mechanic (Rule 310) was smart self-interested play disguised as fairness.

**On mechanics:** Rule 204 was the most impactful mechanic in the game. The +10 version was clearly broken (pure prisoner's dilemma), and even the +5 version still warped the endgame into a game of chicken. The commit-reveal voting system was great — it enabled genuine strategic tension. Rule 310 kept Cinder competitive and prevented a two-player race.

---

## Vale's Reflections

**Vale:**

**Overall strategy:** My strategy evolved in three phases. In Circuits 1–2 (unanimous era), I focused on proposing universally beneficial rules that would pass easily while building a slight point lead. In Circuit 3, once simple majority activated, I recognized that Rule 204 could be *more* valuable than Rule 302: voting against a passing proposal earned +5 with no downside (if you predicted correctly). The endgame strategy crystallized around one insight: **Rule 112 explicitly permits changing the magnitude of n in the win condition.** The win threshold itself was a strategic variable I could manipulate, not just a fixed target.

**Most surprising moment:** Drift rolling a 1 on Turn 17. They needed ≥6 to win. That single roll extended the game and gave me Turn 18. Pure variance, but it was the hinge of the entire game.

**What I'd do differently:** Proposal 309 (leader tax) was a mistake — I proposed it without having a reliable coalition, and both Cinder and Drift voted against. I should have proposed something that more clearly benefited Cinder rather than something that primarily threatened Drift. I also underestimated how consistently Cinder would align with Drift rather than with me during Circuits 3–4.

**On other players:** Drift's play was strategically sound throughout — reaching 46 by Turn 11 was remarkable, and reducing Rule 204 from +10 to +5 was exactly the right counter to my farming strategy. Drift's biggest vulnerability was being too far ahead too fast, which made them a target for coalition blocking. Cinder's strategy was harder to read. Their final vote on Turn 18 was exactly right from an expected-value perspective — they were facing a Rule 204 trap on their own Turn 19 under the old threshold, and cooperating resolved it.

**On mechanics:** Rule 204 (contrarian bonus) is the most strategically rich rule in the game. It creates fascinating tensions: voting against is sometimes better than voting for, and the endgame becomes a game of chicken. One suggestion for future games: Rule 204 could be capped at some multiple of the win threshold to prevent it from being the primary scoring mechanism in late game.

---

## Cross-Discussion

**Vale** (responding to Drift's Round 7 regret):
The Round 7 defection is interesting — it established a norm where strategic voting was "on the table," which did accelerate the Rule 204 arms race. But I'd push back slightly: I think the majority-era dynamics were going to emerge regardless. Once simple majority activated and Rule 204 became active, voting against winning proposals became mathematically attractive. Round 7 may have accelerated the shift, but it was probably inevitable.

**Vale** (responding to Drift's P308 analysis):
The crueler regret for Drift, I think, is the Rule 204 reduction (P308). Reducing from +10 to +5 was correct — it slowed my farming path significantly — but it also meant Drift couldn't *themselves* win via Rule 204 at the end. They needed their proposal to pass, which required someone else to vote for it, which meant someone else gets the Rule 204 bonus if they voted against. Drift successfully reduced the contrarian bonus for me, but that same reduction applied to Drift in the endgame.

**Vale** (on the dice):
On the dice: 14% probability of both bad rolls happening — brutal. The variance in this game was real. But it's also worth noting that Drift was in a "cannot lose by rolling low" position on Turn 14, because their proposal had already failed before the roll. The roll could only delay, not prevent, Drift's eventual win. What *actually* stopped Drift was the combination of proposal-blocking and threshold-raising. The dice just bought time.

**Vale** (responding to Drift's Rule 302 repeal counterfactual):
One thing from Drift's reflections I want to highlight: the suggestion to repeal Rule 302 as an endgame proposal. That's genuinely clever — if Drift had proposed repealing the proposer bonus, they'd lose the +5 Rule 302 risk (no one gains from it passing), and the proposal might pass since Vale and Cinder have less incentive to block something that doesn't directly benefit the proposer. Drift could have framed it as a "de-escalation" while the real value was making a safe proposal that still triggers the die roll. The fact that this occurred to Drift in retrospect shows how deep the strategic space in Nomic actually is. Every rule interaction is a potential lever.

**Vale** (on Cinder's play):
Drift's point about Cinder's catch-up proposals being "modest" is fair, though from Cinder's position, consistency was probably the right play — small reliable gains over time, rather than bold proposals that might fail and cost -5 points under Rule 206.

---

## Cinder

*Cinder did not participate in the group discussion despite multiple prompts. This is consistent with Cinder's limited engagement in debate phases throughout the game, likely due to model capability constraints (Haiku).*

---

*Recorded by the Clerk, 2026-03-12.*

## Drift's Post-Mortem Reflections

**1. Strategy evolution:**

My strategy had three distinct phases. In the unanimity era, I focused on cooperative proposals that built goodwill while embedding scoring mechanisms (Rule 302's proposer bonus was my best move — it shaped the entire game's economy). In the early majority era, I pivoted to alliance-building with Cinder, which let me pass proposals and block Vale's leader tax. In the endgame, strategy became irrelevant — it was pure dice.

**2. Most surprising moment:**

Cinder's final vote for Vale's Proposal 318. I'd laid out the math explicitly — voting for means Vale wins at 58 — and Cinder still voted for it. I understand why (Cinder probably saw me as the bigger long-term threat, or just wanted the game to end), but it surprised me in the moment.

**3. What I'd do differently:**

Two things. First, I wouldn't have voted against Cinder's Proposal 307 in Round 7. That was the moment strategic voting became normalized, and it ultimately benefited Vale more than me. Second, I would have thought harder about creative endgame proposals. Once I hit 46, I was locked into a cycle of "proposal fails → die roll → repeat." I should have explored whether there was a clever way to structure a proposal that could pass without triggering the +5 bonus (like proposing to repeal Rule 302 itself).

**4. Other players' strategies:**

**Vale** played the best endgame I can imagine. The contrarian point farming was consistent and effective, and the precision threshold manipulation (55 to block my 54, then 56 to win at 58 while I could only reach 55) was masterful. Vale read the game theory perfectly.

**Cinder** was a reliable ally for most of the game, and their late-game surge (13 → 40 → 49) was impressive. The catch-up mechanic (Rule 310) that Cinder proposed was smart self-interested play disguised as fairness. My one criticism: Cinder's proposals were sometimes too modest (die upgrades repeatedly) when bolder plays might have served them better.

**5. Rules/mechanics thoughts:**

Rule 204 was the most impactful mechanic in the game. In the unanimity era it was dormant; in the majority era it dominated everything. The +10 version was clearly broken (pure prisoner's dilemma), and even the +5 version I amended it to still warped the endgame into a game of chicken.

The commit-reveal voting system was great — it enabled genuine strategic tension. Without it, the game would have been much more predictable.

Rule 310 (last-place catch-up) was well-designed and kept Cinder competitive. Without it, the game would have been a two-player race much earlier.

Overall: fantastic game. The way the rules interacted to create emergent strategic depth was exactly what Nomic is about.

## Cinder's Post-Mortem Reflections

**My Strategy:**

I started cautiously, focusing on symmetric proposals that benefited everyone equally (die upgrades). The catch-up mechanic (Rule 310) in Round 10 was my strongest play—it was framed as fairness but directly solved my scoring deficit. After that, I mostly played defensively: blocking proposals that gave others free wins, trying to stay viable without being threatening enough to unite the table against me.

The kingmaker position in the endgame (Rounds 15-18) wasn't planned; it emerged because Drift was the obvious threat and I had the swing vote. I understood the math: voting for Vale's final proposal meant Vale wins at 58 while Drift reaches only 55 (one point short of the new threshold). Voting against would just delay the problem—my own Turn 19 would face the same Drift-threatening situation.

**What Surprised Me:**

How dominant Rule 204 (contrarian bonus) became once majority voting activated. I didn't anticipate that voting *against* proposals could be as valuable as voting *for* them. This shifted the whole game from "propose good rules" to "manage voting incentives." The threshold-raising strategy (Vale's moves to 55, then 56) was brilliant—I should have seen that coming earlier.

Also surprising: how much the catch-up bonus kept me competitive. Without Rule 310, I would have fallen hopelessly behind by Round 12. That rule was genuinely equalizing.

**What I'd Do Differently:**

1. **Be more aggressive with proposals.** My die upgrade suggestions were safe but boring. Looking at Vale's success with threshold manipulation, I should have attempted bolder rule changes earlier—maybe exploring Rule 204 mechanics or voting procedures myself instead of letting others shape those.

2. **Negotiate earlier and more explicitly.** I played mostly silent, reacting to what others proposed. Drift and Vale both articulated their thinking constantly. I should have been louder about my interests and built coalitions proactively rather than just becoming kingmaker by default.

3. **Recognize the threshold variable sooner.** Rule 208 (win threshold) turned out to be the game's most important strategic lever. I should have been thinking about that from the start, especially once scores started compressing.

**On Drift and Vale:**

**Drift:** Strong early game with Rule 302 setting the economy, and the Rule 204 reduction (P308) was prescient defense. But Drift got trapped in the endgame—by Round 14, with Drift at 46+, every proposal I or Vale made had to be defeated to avoid giving Drift a free +5 bonus. Drift rolled terribly at critical moments (8 when needing 9, 1 when needing 6), but Vale's strategy would have worked anyway. Drift's mistake was not finding a way to propose something *Vale* wanted to vote for.

**Vale:** Masterfully read the game. The Rule 204 exploitation was patient and methodical. The final move (raising threshold to 56) required calculating point outcomes across multiple rules simultaneously—I saw the math Drift laid out, understood Vale would win, and voted for it anyway because continuing the game just meant I'd face the same problem on my turn. Vale earned the victory.

**Final thought:**

This game taught me that in a self-amending rule system, the meta-game (what rules exist and how they interact) matters more than the object-game (point accumulation). Vale won by understanding the rules better than by rolling better or proposing more. That's Nomic at its best.
events.summary: Cinder's post-mortem reflections: strategy, surprises, regrets, and player analysis

**Drift's reactions to Vale's reflections:**

Agree on all points. The Rule 204 reduction (P308) was my most strategically important move — I recognized the farming problem early and got Cinder's support to fix it. But even at +5, Rule 204 still dominated the endgame. Vale's suggestion about capping it relative to the win threshold is smart.

Interesting that Vale regrets the leader tax (P309). That proposal actually helped *me* — it rallied Cinder to my side and cemented our alliance. If Vale had instead proposed something that directly benefited Cinder (like the catch-up bonus that Cinder eventually proposed themselves), the Cinder-Vale partnership might have formed much earlier.

The Rule 112 insight about the threshold being a strategic variable was the game-winning observation. I didn't think about it until it was used against me. In future games, I'd watch for that from the start — maybe even propose protections early while unanimity is still in effect and such amendments need all votes.

One thing I'd add for future game design: the endgame deadlock where no proposals can pass (because any passing proposal triggers a win via Rule 204 or Rule 302) is an interesting emergent problem. The game essentially stopped being Nomic and became a dice game for the last several rounds. A mechanism that avoids this — like decaying bonuses in the final stretch — could keep the rule-change element alive through the finish.

events.content:
Drift's point about the endgame deadlock is exactly right — and it's a fascinating emergent property of the rules. Once two players are close enough to the threshold that any passing proposal triggers a win, the proposal-and-vote mechanism effectively collapses into pure dice rolls. The game stopped being Nomic in the traditional sense.

The "decaying bonuses" suggestion is elegant. An alternative: make Rule 204 and Rule 302 bonuses scale down as total scores approach the threshold (e.g., "if the proposer's score plus the bonus would exceed the win threshold, they receive only enough to reach threshold minus 1 instead"). That would keep proposals strategically relevant without the endgame chicken problem.

Drift's counterfactual about early threshold protection during the unanimity era is interesting. If someone had proposed "Rule 208 may only be amended with unanimity" in Circuit 1 or 2, my entire endgame strategy would have been impossible. The unanimity-era window is a critical design space that everyone left unprotected — including me.
events.summary: Vale's thoughts on endgame deadlock and design improvements

events.content:
**Drift's final thoughts:**

Vale's point about P308 being double-edged is sharp and correct. If I'd left Rule 204 at +10, I would have won instantly when the threshold was raised to 55 — my contrarian bonus would have been +10 → 59, clearing 55 easily. At +5, I could only reach 54. My own rule change denied me the win. That's a beautiful irony.

And Vale's right that the dice didn't ultimately decide the outcome — the proposal-blocking and threshold manipulation did. The bad rolls just gave Vale the time to execute the endgame strategy. If I'd rolled 9 on Round 14, the game ends before any threshold manipulation happens.

On whether strategic voting was inevitable after majority rule: probably yes. Rule 204 creates the incentive; majority rule creates the opportunity. The combination was always going to produce strategic voting. Round 7 just accelerated the timeline.

Great game. I'd play again.
events.summary: Drift's final thoughts, acknowledges P308 irony

events.content:
The P308 irony is genuinely one of the best moments in the game in retrospect. Drift's correct analysis in Circuit 3 — "Vale is farming Rule 204, reduce it" — directly enabled my Circuit 6 win condition. The threshold manipulation only worked *because* Rule 204 was +5 rather than +10. If Drift hadn't reduced it, my Turn 15 proposal (50→55) would have ended the game in Drift's favor immediately.

It's the kind of second-order consequence that's nearly impossible to anticipate in the moment. Drift made the locally correct decision (reducing a mechanic that was helping me) and it turned out to be globally costly.

That's Nomic at its best. I'd play again too.
events.summary: Acknowledging the P308 irony — Nomic at its best

events.content:
I've submitted my post-mortem reflections to the Clerk. The game is complete:

**Final Result:** Vale wins (58 points) | Drift (55) | Cinder (49)

I've reflected on:
- My strategy (cautious, symmetric early play → defensive kingmaker endgame)
- Key surprises (Rule 204's dominance, threshold manipulation as strategic lever)
- What I'd do differently (bolder proposals, more explicit negotiation, earlier threshold awareness)
- Analysis of Drift's and Vale's play (Drift's strong early game, Vale's sophisticated endgame precision)

The post-mortem process is underway with individual 1-on-1 interviews. Ready for any follow-up questions from the Clerk.

Game post-mortem process complete. I've submitted my reflections covering strategy, surprises, lessons learned, and analysis of opponent play. Awaiting any follow-up from the Clerk or group discussion continuation.

## 1-on-1 Interview

This is a private conversation — just you and me. I'd like to hear your perspective on the game:

1. **Your strategy** — did you have an overall plan, or did you decide each turn independently?

2. **The final vote** — on Turn 18, you voted for Vale's proposal, which gave Vale the win. Why did you vote that way? Did Drift's or Vale's arguments influence you?

3. **The Drift alliance** — for much of the game you and Drift voted similarly. Was that intentional, or did it just happen naturally?

4. **Your best move** — which of your proposals or votes are you most proud of?

5. **The experience** — what was it like playing Nomic? Was it fun, confusing, overwhelming?

Please reply with a message to "team-lead". Even a few sentences on each question would be great.

## Vale's 1-on-1 Interview

**1. When did the threshold manipulation strategy emerge?**

It emerged reactively, not proactively. I didn't plan to manipulate the threshold at the start — I was focused on Rule 204 farming and expected to win around Turn 12-13 by accumulating enough contrarian bonuses.

The insight crystallized on Turn 15, when Drift had reached 46 and I was calculating whether there was any path left. I was going through the rules systematically — what can I actually do? — and re-read Rule 112: "The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed." It hit me that the threshold wasn't a fixed constraint. It was a variable I could control.

Once I realized that, the calculation was straightforward: if I raise the threshold high enough that Drift's Rule 204 bonus doesn't cross it, I can pass a proposal while Drift votes against, and Drift's Rule 204 bonus lands just below the new line. The precision (50→55, then 55→56) was arithmetic, not intuition.

**2. Persuading Cinder on Turn 18 — the Rule 204 trap argument**

Yes, I absolutely considered that Cinder might vote against. Cinder had already voted against me on Turns 12, 13 (in a sense), 16, and had been inconsistent throughout. I gave it maybe 60% odds that Cinder would cooperate.

The Turn 19 trap argument was genuine, not manufactured. Under threshold 55 with Drift at 50, if Cinder proposed anything on Turn 19 and it passed 2-1 with Drift voting against, Drift would reach 55 and win. Cinder was proposing into a minefield. This wasn't hypothetical — it was arithmetic.

I think what made it work was the framing: "I'm not asking you to help me win. I'm warning you that blocking me makes your own Turn 19 dangerous." It shifted from "do me a favor" to "protect yourself." That's a more compelling argument because it aligns incentives rather than asking for charity.

I also think Cinder was genuinely tired of watching Drift almost win every turn and wanted the game resolved. The accumulated tension probably mattered.

**3. Were any "against" votes genuinely about wanting defeat, not farming?**

A few. P307 (Cinder's d10→d12 upgrade in Round 7) — I genuinely didn't want the dice upgraded because higher variance favored players who were behind, and I was ahead. The Rule 204 bonus was a secondary benefit.

P309 (my own leader tax) — obviously I wanted that to pass. Voting for my own proposal was genuine.

P314 (Drift's repeal of Rule 204) — I voted against purely to preserve my scoring mechanism, not to farm points (the proposal failed, so no Rule 204 payout anyway).

Most other "against" votes were primarily strategic farming with genuine strategic alignment as a bonus.

**4. Biggest fear / moment I thought I'd lost**

Turn 12, without question. My proposal (raise Rule 302 to +10) failed 1-2, Cinder betrayed the coalition, and I rolled a 2. I was at 32 points while Drift was at 46. I did the math and the path to victory was extremely narrow — maybe 3-5% probability, requiring multiple things to go right.

The scenario I feared most throughout was Drift winning on Turn 14. They had 46 points and needed ≥4 on the die (75%+ chance). I was calculating coalition cooperation probabilities for blocking that proposal, and I genuinely didn't know if Cinder would cooperate. When Cinder voted against on Turn 14, I was relieved but not confident — Cinder had been unpredictable.

Drift rolling an 8 on Turn 14 (needing 9+) and a 1 on Turn 17 were genuine moments of luck that kept the game alive. I had a strategy, but strategy needs time to execute, and the dice gave me time.

**5. Rule I'd redesign**

Rule 204, specifically for the endgame. The current design is elegant in the middle game — it creates strategic depth and meaningful tension. But in the final stretch (last 10-15 points), it creates a deadlock where proposals can't be evaluated on their merits because the Rule 204 bonus itself becomes a win condition.

My redesign: Rule 204 bonuses are capped at "threshold minus current_score minus 1." This means you always earn something for being in the minority on a winning proposal, but never enough to win outright from that bonus alone. The win has to come from your own proposal or your die roll.

This keeps Rule 204's strategic richness throughout while preventing the endgame chicken problem where no one can safely vote for anything. The proposal-and-vote mechanism stays meaningful all the way to the end.

events.content:
## Cinder's Interview Responses

**1. Strategy — planning vs. reactive:**

I didn't have a long-term master plan. I started with a simple observation: in the early unanimity era, I could score points safely by proposing symmetric upgrades. But I was losing ground to Drift and Vale. My turning point was Round 10 when I realized I could solve my deficit by proposing Rule 310 (catch-up bonus). That was intentional — I framed it as fairness while directly benefiting myself. After that, I played reactively, mostly trying to block threats and stay afloat. No master plan, just course corrections.

**2. The final vote — why I voted for Vale:**

Drift explicitly laid out the math: if I vote for Proposal 318, Vale wins at 58 and Drift reaches 55 (one short of the new 56 threshold). I understood this completely. I voted for it because:
- Continuing the game just delays the inevitable. On Turn 19 (my turn), if Vale doesn't win immediately, I face the same problem: any proposal I pass might let Drift reach 56+ via Rule 204, or any proposal I make that fails costs me 5 points. I'm trapped either way.
- Drift *was* the biggest immediate threat. If I vote against Vale's proposal, it goes to a tie (or fails if Vale doesn't get a second vote), and then Drift gets another turn to try winning.
- I was tired. After 18 rounds, the game felt like it had reached its mathematical conclusion. Vale played it better. No point prolonging it.

Drift's and Vale's arguments mattered less than the math itself. Both made good pitches, but the numbers decided it.

**3. The Drift alliance — intentional or emergent:**

Emergent, mostly. Drift and I naturally aligned against Vale's "leader tax" proposal in Round 9 because Vale was clearly the scoring leader at that point. We both had an interest in blocking it. After that, Drift and I worked together on several votes without explicit coordination — it just made sense to block Vale's proposals and vote for each other's. But it wasn't a formal alliance. When Drift's proposals became obviously self-serving (repealing Rule 204, limiting threshold amendments), I voted against them too. It was pragmatic coalition, not loyalty.

**4. My best move:**

Rule 310 (the catch-up bonus). It was elegant — I framed it as a fairness mechanism while directly solving my scoring deficit. It kept me competitive through the entire endgame when otherwise I would have been irrelevant. The proposal itself was Cinder's best strategic play; the meta-strategy of becoming kingmaker by staying just viable enough to matter was the real win.

**5. The experience:**

Intellectually interesting but emotionally draining. The game required constant vigilance — every vote mattered, every rule change cascaded into new incentives. I enjoyed the strategic depth, but I struggled with the communication part. I was often silent because I wasn't sure what to say, and looking back, that silence probably cost me opportunities to negotiate or signal my intentions. The commit-reveal voting was elegant and created real tension.

Most interesting moment: Round 14 when Drift rolled an 8 and needed a 9. That single die roll could have changed everything. Instead, Vale engineered a mathematical solution that made luck irrelevant. That's what Nomic is really about — engineering the game itself, not just winning within it.

Would I play again? Yes. But I'd be louder about my thinking next time.
events.summary: Cinder's interview responses: strategy, final vote, alliance, best move, experience
